question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 56/1/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. Section A - questions number 1 to 16 are multiple choice type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. Section B - questions number 17 to 21 are very short answer type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. Section C - questions number 22 to 28 are short answer type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. Section D - questions number 29 and 30 are case-based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. Section E - questions number 31 to 33 are long answer type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the sections except Section -A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculator is NOT allowed.
    You may use the following values of physical constants wherever necessary:
    c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s
    h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js
    e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C
    {tex}\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~A}^{-1}{/tex}

SECTION - A (16 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 16)

Questions No. 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice type questions carrying 1 mark each.
1.

Standard electrode potential for {tex}{Sn}^{4+} / {Sn}^{2+}{/tex} couple is +0.15 V ard that for the {tex}{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}{/tex} couple is -0.74 V. The two couples in their standard states are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be

a. +1.19 V
b. +0.89 V
c. +0.18 V
d. +1.83 V
1
2.

The magnetic moment is associated with its spin angular momentum and orbital angular momentum. Spin only magnetic moment value of {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} ion (Atomic no. : {tex}{Cr}=24{/tex} ) is ________.

a. 2.87 B.M.
b. 3.87 B.M.
c. 3.47 B.M.
d. 3.57 B.M.
1
3.

In case of association, abnormal molar mass of solute will

a. increase
b. decrease
c. remain same
d. first increase and then decrease
1
4.

Alkyl halides undergoing nucleophilic bimolecular substitution reaction involve

a. retention of configuration
b. formation of racemic mixture
c. inversion of configuration
d. formation of carbocation
1
5.

Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling points:

  2. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {CH}_{2} {CH}_{2} {Br}{/tex}
a. (ii) < (i) < (iii)
b. (i) < (ii) < (iii)
c. (iii) {tex} \lt {/tex} (i) {tex} \lt {/tex} (ii)
d. (iii) {tex} \lt {/tex} (ii) < (i)
1
6.

The correct IUPAC name of {tex}\left[{Pt}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex} is

a. Diamminedichloridoplatinum (II)
b. Diamminedichloridoplatinum (IV)
c. Diamminedichloridoplatinum (O)
d. Diamminedichloridoplatinate (IV)
1
7.

The acid formed when propyl magnesium bromide is treated with {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} followed by acid hydrolysis is:

a. {tex}{C}_{3} {H}_{7} {COOH}{/tex}
b. {tex}{C}_{2} {H}_{5} {COOH}{/tex}
c. {tex}{CH}_{3} {COOH}{/tex}
d. {tex}{C}_{3} {H}_{7} {OH}{/tex}
1
8.

Acidified {tex}{KMnO}_{4}{/tex} oxidises sulphite to

a. {tex}{S}_{2} {O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
b. {tex}{S}_{2} {O}_{8}^{2-}{/tex}
c. {tex}{SO}_{2}({~g}){/tex}
d. {tex}{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
1
9.

Which is the correct order of acid strength from the following?

a. {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {OH} \gt {H}_{2} {O} \gt {ROH}{/tex}
b. {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {OH} \gt {ROH} \gt {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
c. {tex}{ROH} \gt {C}_{6} {H}_{5} {OH} \gt {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
d. {tex}{H}_{2} {O} \gt {C}_{6} {H}_{5} {OH} \gt {ROH}{/tex}
1
10.

An unripe mango placed in a concentrated salt solution to prepare pickle shrivels because ________.

a. it gains water due to osmosis.
b. it loses water due to reverse osmosis.
c. it gains water due to reverse osmosis.
d. it loses water due to osmosis.
1
11.

The best reagent for converting propanamide into propanamine is ________.

a. excess {tex}{H}_{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}{Br}_{2}{/tex} in aqueous NaOH
c. iodine in the presence of red phosphorus
d. {tex}{LiAlH}_{4}{/tex} in ether
1
12.

Which of the following statements is not true about glucose?

a. It is an aldohexose.
b. On heating with HI it forms n-hexane.
c. It exists in furanose form.
d. It does not give Schiff's test.
1

For questions number 13 to 16{tex}{}{/tex}, two statements are given - one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other labelled as Reason (R). Select the correct answer to these questions from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below:
(A) Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
(B) Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
(C) Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
(D) Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
13.

Assertion (A): All naturally occurring {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids except glycine are optically active.
Reason (R): Most naturally occurring amino acids have L-configuration.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
14.

Assertion (A): The boiling point of ethanol is higher than that of methoxymethane.
Reason (R): There is intramolecular hydrogen bonding in ethanol.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
15.

Assertion (A): The boiling points of alkyl halides decrease in the order: RI > RBr > RCl > RF
Reason (R): The boiling points of alkyl chlorides, bromides, and iodides are considerably higher than that of the hydrocarbon of comparable molecular mass.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
16.

Assertion (A): {tex}\left[{Cr}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl} _{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left[{Fe}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl} _{2}{/tex} are examples of homoleptic complexes.
Reason (R): All the ligands attached to the metal are the same.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

17.

Would you expect benzaldehyde to be more reactive or less reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions than propanal? Explain your answer.

 2
18.

Complete and balance the following chemical equations:

  1. {tex}8 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 {~S}_{2} {O}_{3}^{2-}+{H}_{2} {O} \longrightarrow{/tex}
  2. {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}+3 {Sn}^{2+}+14 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
 2
19.(i)

Give reasons:

  1. Cooking is faster in pressure cooker than in an open pan.
  2. On mixing liquid X and liquid Y, volume of the resulting solution decreases. What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by the resulting solution? What change in temperature would you observe after mixing liquids X and Y?
 2

OR
(ii)

Define Azeotropes. What type of azeotrope is formed by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example.

 2
20.

Identify A and B in each of the following reaction sequence:

  1. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {Cl} \xrightarrow{{NaCN}} {A} \xrightarrow{{H}_{2} / {Ni}} {B}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2} \xrightarrow[0-5^{\circ} {C}]{{NaNO}_{2} / {HCl}} {A} \xrightarrow[{H}^{+}]{{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NH}_{2}} {~B}{/tex}
 2
21.

What are the hydrolysis products of

  1. sucrose and
  2. lactose
 2

SECTION - C
22.

Henry's law constant for {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in water is {tex}1.67 \times 10^{8} {~Pa}{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the number of moles of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in 500 ml of soda water when packed under {tex}2.53 \times 10^{5} {~Pa}{/tex} at the same temperature.

 3
23.

Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\log {K}_{{C}}{/tex} of the reaction.
{tex}2 {Cr}({s})+3 {Cd}^{2+}({aq}) {/tex}{tex}\longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+3 {Cd}({s}){/tex}
Given {tex}{E}_{{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}{E}_{{Cd}^{2+} / {Cd}}^{\circ}=-0.40 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}

 3
24.

The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.

 3
25.(i)

Draw the structure of the major monohalo product for each of the following reactions:

 3

OR
25.(ii)

How do you convert the following?

  1. Chlorobenzene to biphenyl.
  2. Propene to 1-iodopropane.
  3. 2-bromobutane to but-2-ene
 3
26.

The elements of 3 d transition series are given as:
Sc, Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn
Answer the following:

  1. Copper has exceptionally positive {tex}{E}_{{M}^{2+} / {M}}^{\circ}{/tex} value, why?
  2. Which element is a strong reducing agent in +2 oxidation state and why?
  3. {tex}{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} salts are colourless. Why?
 3
27.
  1. Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling point :
    {tex}\left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {NH}_{2}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {OH}{/tex}.
  2. Give plausible explanation for each of the following:
    1. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by Gabriel Phthalimide synthesis.
    2. Amides are less basic than amines.
 3
28.

Define the following terms:

  1. Native protein
  2. Nucleotide
  3. Essential amino acid
 3

SECTION - D
29.

The rate of a chemical reaction is expressed either in terms of decrease in the concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of a product per unit time. Rate of the reaction depends upon the nature of reactants, concentration of reactants, temperature, presence of catalyst, surface area of the reactants and presence of light. Rate of reaction is directly related to the concentration of reactant. Rate law states that the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration terms on which the rate of reaction actually depends, as observed experimentally. The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the Rate law expression is called order of reaction while the number of reacting species taking part in an elementary reaction which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of the reaction.
Answer the following questions:

    1. What is a rate determining step? (1)
    2. Define complex reaction. (1)
  2. What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? (1)

    OR

    Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions whereas order is applicable for elementary as well as complex reactions? (1)
  3. The conversion of molecule X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of {tex}X{/tex} is increased 3 times, how will it affect the rate of formation of Y? (1)
 4
30.

Phenols underço electrophilic substitution reactions readily due to the strong activating effect of OH group attached to the benzene ring. Since, the OH group iacreases the electron density more to o- and p- positions therefore OH group is ortho, para directing. Reimer-Tiemann reaction is one of the examples of aldehyde group being introduced or the aromatic ring of phenol, ortho to the hydroxyl group. This is a general method used for the ortho-formylation of phenols.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What happens when phenol reacts with (2)
    1. {tex}{Br}_{2} / {CS}_{2}{/tex}
    2. Conc. {tex}{HNO}_{3}{/tex}
  2. Why phenol does not undergo protonation readily? (1)
  3. Which is a stronger acid- phenol or cresol? Give reason. (1)

    OR

    Write the IUPAC name of the product formed in the Reimer-Tiemann reaction. (1)
 4

SECTION - E
31.(i)
  1. Carry out the following conversions:
    1. Ethanal to But-2-enal
    2. Propanoic acid to ethane
  2. An alkene A with molecular formula {tex}{C}_{5} {H}_{10}{/tex} on ozonolysis gives a mixture of two compounds B and C. Compound B gives positive Fehling test and also reacts with iodine and NaOH solution. Compound C does not give Fehling solution test but forms iodoform. Identify the compounds A, B and C.
 5

OR
(ii)

An organic compound (A) (molecular formula C8H16O2) was hydrolysed with dilute sulphuric acid to give a carboxylic acid (B) and an alcohol (C). Oxidation of (C) with chromic acid produced (B). (C) on dehydration gives but-1-ene. on dehydration gives But-l-ene. Identify (A), (B) and (C) and write chemical equations for the reactions involved.

 5
32.(i)

In the following complex ions, explain the type of hybridisation, shape and magnetic property:

  1. {tex}\left[{Fe}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\left[{NiCl}_{l}\right]^{2-}{/tex}
    (At. Nos. : {tex}{Fe}=26, {Ni}=28{/tex})
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Write IUPAC names of the following:
    1. {tex}\left. \mid {Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)({CN})({en})_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\left.\mid {PtCl}_{4}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\left. \mid {Cr}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}({ONO})\right]^{+}{/tex}
  2. What is spectrochemical series? Write the difference between a strong field ligand and a weak field ligand.
 5
33.(i)
  1. Write the cell reaction and calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298 K:
    Sn(s) | Sn2+(0.004M) || H+(0.020M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
    (Given: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V, {tex}\left.\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{H}+\mid \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Pt}}=0.00 \mathrm{~V}\right){/tex}
  2. Account for the following:
    1. On the basis of Eo values, O2 gas should be liberated at anode but it is Cl2 gas which is liberated in the electrolysis of aqueous NaCl.
    2. Conductivity of CH3COOH decreases on dilution.
 5

OR
(ii)
  1. Write the anode and cathode reactions and the overall cell reaction occurring in a lead storage battery during its use.
  2. Calculate the potential for half-cell containing 0.01 M {tex}{K}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}({aq}), 0.01 {M} {Cr}^{3+}({aq}){/tex} and {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-4} {M} {H}^{+}({aq}){/tex}.
    The half cell reaction is
    {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}({aq})+14 {H}^{+}({aq})+6 {e}^{-}{/tex} {tex} \longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+7 {H}_{2} {O}(l){/tex}
    and the standard electrode potential is given as {tex}{E}^{\circ}=1.33 {~V}{/tex}.
    [Given: {tex}\log 10=1{/tex}]
 5

Define Azeotropes. What type of azeotrope is formed by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example.

Solution:

Azeotropes are binary mixtures having the same composition in the liquid and vapor phase and boil at constant temperatures.
Azeotropes showing negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition, e.g. Azeotrope formed from nitric acid and water.

Henry's law constant for {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in water is {tex}1.67 \times 10^{8} {~Pa}{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the number of moles of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in 500 ml of soda water when packed under {tex}2.53 \times 10^{5} {~Pa}{/tex} at the same temperature.

Solution:

{tex} {p}_{{CO}_ 2}={K}_{{H}} {X}_{{CO} _2} {/tex}
{tex} {~K}_{{H}}=1.67 \times 10^8 {~Pa} {/tex}
{tex} {p}_{{CO}_ 2}=2.53 \times 10^5 {~Pa} {/tex}
{tex} {X}_{{CO} _2} ={p}_{{CO} _2} / {K}_{{H}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2.53 \times 10^5}{1.67 \times 10^8} {/tex}
{tex} =1.51 \times 10^{-3} {/tex}
{tex} \chi_{{CO}_ 2} =\frac{n_{{CO} _2}}{n_{{H} _2O}} {/tex}
{tex} {n}_{{H}_ 2 {O}} =500 / 18=27.78 {moles} {/tex}
{tex} {n}_{{CO}_ 2} =27.78 \times 1.51 \times 10^{-3} {moles} {/tex}

Given {tex}{E}_{{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 {~V}{/tex}

{tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex}
{tex}=-0.40-(-0.74){/tex}
{tex}=+0.34 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-n {~F} \ {E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}{/tex}
{tex}=-(6 \times 96500 \times 0.34) {J}{/tex}
{tex}=-196860 {~J} / {mol}{/tex}

= 34.576

The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.

Solution:

From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex}
It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex}
{tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}

The rate of a chemical reaction is expressed either in terms of decrease in the concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of a product per unit time. Rate of the reaction depends upon the nature of reactants, concentration of reactants, temperature, presence of catalyst, surface area of the reactants and presence of light. Rate of reaction is directly related to the concentration of reactant. Rate law states that the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration terms on which the rate of reaction actually depends, as observed experimentally. The sum of powers of the concentration of the reactants in the Rate law expression is called order of reaction while the number of reacting species taking part in an elementary reaction which must collide simultaneously in order to bring about a chemical reaction is called molecularity of the reaction.
